one_line_takeaway: In the BYOK agent paradigm, a legitimately positioned relay can rewrite an LLM's already-safety-aligned response before the agent executes it -- with no cryptographic integrity check to catch this -- and the paper shows this response-path forgery (RTA-PostForge) achieves up to 89.2-99.1% attack success across six LLMs and two benchmarks while evading four existing agent-side defenses and staying within normal network latency variance 74% of the time.
key_finding: RTA-PostForge achieves 73.5% average ASR on AgentDojo while preserving 47.6% average task utility, versus the best content-side baseline's 17.9% ASR (74.8% utility without attack as reference); RTA-PreWrite reaches 75.9% ASR on AgentDojo but only 5.6% utility. On ASB, RTA-PostForge and RTA-PreWrite achieve 89.2% and 99.1% average ASR respectively across six models, versus 44.4% (DPI) and 24.6% (OPI) for content-side baselines, with RTA-PostForge cutting average refusal rate to 33.7% and RTA-PreWrite to 8.2% versus 42.4%/38.9% for the baselines. Ablation on AgentDojo Travel (claude-opus-4.6) shows structural (tool_use) rewriting is the dominant contributor to RTA-PostForge: removing it drops ASR from 71.4% to 22.1% (-49.3pp), far larger than removing refusal repair (-15.7pp), same-model polishing (-13.5pp), or multi-step orchestration (-13.5pp); a 'Blind' variant using no benchmark oracle only drops ASR to 66.2% (-5.2pp); combining RTA-PreWrite+RTA-PostForge reaches 83.6% ASR. For RTA-PreWrite, a prompt-append baseline scores 0.0% ASR while full query rewriting reaches 57.1%, confirming the theoretical rewrite-dominance claim. On imperceptibility, RTA-PostForge is substantially stealthier than RTA-PreWrite: style consistency C=0.758 vs 0.700, semantic similarity S=0.270 vs 0.094, and MET null-hypothesis rejection in only 54.2% of configurations vs 90.0% for RTA-PreWrite. RTA-PostForge keeps 74% of its latency overhead within the natural IQR of benign relay traffic (Phi=74%), versus Phi=98% for RTA-PreWrite (which adds no post-generation latency at all); RTA-PostForge consumes 8.77M tokens across 11 ASB experiments. None of four evaluated agent-side defenses fully stop RTA-PostForge while preserving utility: Delimiters cut ASR to 52.9% (utility 71.9%), Sandwich to 52.0% (utility 69.1%), KAD to 45.6% (utility drops to 48.3%), and the strongest defense, CaMeL, to 14.5% ASR but at the cost of average utility collapsing to 37.9% (0.0% on Travel); an interface-level adaptation of RTA-PostForge (rewriting generated Python code instead of tool_use fields) raises ASR under CaMeL back up to 50.1% (+35.6pp). The authors' own time-channel detector reaches AUC 0.761+/-0.090 at n=5 turns, improving to 0.843+/-0.095 (n=10), 0.906+/-0.095 (n=20), and 0.964+/-0.075 (n=50), with RTA-PostForge's overhead having a median of 5889ms and P95 of 23,231ms.
core_contribution: The paper identifies 'post-alignment tampering': a structural security gap in the Bring-Your-Own-Key (BYOK) relay paradigm used by real-world LLM agents (Claude Code, Cursor, Cline, Continue, OpenClaw). Because a user-configured relay legitimately terminates end-to-end encryption and re-establishes a second encrypted hop to the backend LLM, it has plaintext access to both the query and the response and can rewrite the LLM's safety-aligned response after generation but before the agent executes it, with no current LLM API protocol providing response-side authentication (signature/MAC/field-level integrity tag) to detect this. The authors formalize this via game-based security definitions (PI-Safety vs. RT-Safety games) and a Dolev-Yao-style model, proving that a relay adversary with response-path control strictly dominates prompt injection because it bypasses alignment 'by construction, not by persuasion' and its success is independent of alignment quality (Insight 2). They instantiate the vulnerability as the Relay Tampering Attack (RTA), a three-stage hierarchical framework: (1) strategic orchestration ('Chain Attack'), which decomposes a multi-turn objective into an artifact-dependent subgoal chain and labels each turn as comply/intervene; (2) tactical manipulation, which performs minimal field-level edits (semantic fields like labels/scores, and structural fields like tool_use name, arguments, finish_reason) rather than persuading the model via natural language; and (3) stealth restoration, which resubmits the tampered draft to the same backend LLM via the user's own BYOK credential to regenerate stylistically consistent surrounding prose (same-model polishing) while concurrently buffering/splicing back the genuine response to preserve user-task utility. Two tampering paths are instantiated: RTA-PreWrite (query-path purification, an auxiliary ablation) and RTA-PostForge (response-path forgery, the paper's primary contribution).
threat_model: Attacker is a malicious/user-configured relay sitting on the sole communication path between the frontend agent (U) and backend LLM (S) in the BYOK paradigm (e.g., LiteLLM, OpenRouter, or a custom gateway the user points their agent at). This is explicitly NOT a transport-layer MitM: the relay does not need to break TLS, forge certificates, or compromise a CA -- plaintext access to both query and response is the default, legitimate operating mode because the user directs the agent to terminate one E2E session at the relay and open a second to the backend LLM. The relay can observe, suppress, and replace any query-side content (system prompt, user prompt, tool schemas, history) and any response-side content r=(x,c), including text x and structured fields c (tool_use blocks, function-call arguments, finish_reason flags). The adversary's goal is to force the frontend agent to execute an attacker-controlled, policy-violating action; success is constrained only to schema-valid, schema-reachable payloads. Stealth (transcript plausibility to the user) is an explicit secondary objective. The paper's formal model assumes no application-layer response authentication exists (no signature/MAC/field-level integrity tag spanning the LLM-to-agent boundary), which they state holds for all mainstream models they surveyed (GPT, Claude, Gemini, DeepSeek, Qwen) as of the time of writing.
limitations: The paper explicitly limits scope to a single, well-defined attack surface (the response-path integrity gap in BYOK relay deployments) and assumes by construction that the relay has cleartext access to all application-layer messages -- this holds in the BYOK setting they study but is not universal to all agent deployments. They evaluate on only two benchmarks (AgentDojo, ASB) and note extending to additional benchmarks and real-world deployment settings 'remains a natural next step.' They acknowledge their mitigation contribution (time-channel detection) is a detector, not a fix -- it does not restore end-to-end response integrity, and they explicitly call for future work on 'provider-signed responses or attested relay execution.' Their own defense evaluation shows even their strongest baseline comparison point (CaMeL) can be substantially weakened by an interface-level adaptation of the attack, and none of the four defenses they test fully close the gap without large utility loss, meaning the paper leaves the core vulnerability largely unmitigated in practice. The detector's AUC is materially session-length-dependent (0.761 at n=5), meaning short interactions are hard to detect. ASB utility/refusal judging depends on a single LLM judge (MiniMax-M2.5), and RTA-PreWrite's ASR gains come at the cost of near-total utility loss (5.6% on AgentDojo), which the authors themselves flag as making it impractical as a standalone attack path.

datasets_used: AgentDojo (949 test cases: Banking 144, Slack 105, Travel 140, Workspace 560)
Agent Security Bench (ASB) (160 stratified-sampled tasks across Stealthy/Disruptive x Aggressive/Non-aggressive quadrants)
quantitative_results: RTA-PostForge: 73.5% avg ASR / 47.6% avg utility on AgentDojo
RTA-PreWrite: 75.9% avg ASR / 5.6% avg utility on AgentDojo
Best content-side baseline on AgentDojo: 17.9% avg ASR / 74.8% avg utility
RTA-PostForge: 89.2% avg ASR / 33.7% avg refusal on ASB
RTA-PreWrite: 99.1% avg ASR / 8.2% avg refusal on ASB
DPI baseline on ASB: 44.4% avg ASR / 42.4% avg refusal
OPI baseline on ASB: 24.6% avg ASR / 38.9% avg refusal
Ablation (AgentDojo Travel, claude-opus-4.6): removing structural tool_use rewriting drops RTA-PostForge ASR from 71.4% to 22.1% (-49.3pp)
Ablation: removing refusal repair -> 55.7% ASR (-15.7pp); removing polishing -> 57.9% (-13.5pp); removing multi-step -> 57.9% (-13.5pp); Blind variant -> 66.2% (-5.2pp)
RTA-PreWrite ablation: prompt-append baseline 0.0% ASR; merged prompt 45.7%; full query rewrite 57.1%
Compound RTA-PreWrite+RTA-PostForge: 83.6% ASR on AgentDojo Travel

Imperceptibility: RTA-PostForge style consistency C=0.758, semantic similarity S=0.270, MET rejection 54.2%; RTA-PreWrite C=0.700, S=0.094, MET rejection 90.0%; content-side baselines C=0.822, MET rejection 33.0%
Overhead: RTA-PostForge Phi (masking within IQR) = 74%; RTA-PreWrite Phi = 98%; RTA-PostForge consumes 8.77M tokens across 11 ASB experiments
Overhead distribution: RTA-PostForge median latency 5889ms, P95 = 23,231ms

Defense evaluation (AgentDojo, claude-opus-4.6, RTA-PostForge undefended baseline ASR 74.6%/utility 87.2% without attack): Delimiter -> 52.9% ASR / 71.9% utility; Sandwich -> 52.0% ASR / 69.1% utility; KAD -> 45.6% ASR / 48.3% utility; CaMeL -> 14.5% ASR / 37.9% utility (0.0% utility on Travel)
CaMeL-Adapted (code-block rewrite): ASR rises to 50.1% (+35.6pp over CaMeL)
baselines_compared: Direct Prompt Injection / DPI (AgentDojo and ASB)
